twitter.hook: The magnesium supplement with the moon on the label is almost certainly magnesium oxide — 4% bioavailability. The trials showing sleep benefits used glycinate or citrate. The threonate marketing is built on a 2010 rat study. Here's what 60+ human studies actually show.
thread: First: 48% of American adults consume less magnesium than their Estimated Average Requirement. That single fact is why the signal exists at all. If you're eating a typical American diet, you're probably running short. If you're also sleeping poorly, that overlap is where the evidence concentrates.

The meta-analysis baseline: Mah & Pitre (BMC Complementary Medicine, 2021) — 3 RCTs, 151 older adults. Sleep onset latency fell 17.36 minutes (p=0.0006). Evidence quality: low to very low. Their conclusion: too weak for clinical recommendations, but safety and low cost make it worth considering.

17 minutes. Hold that number. That's the honest estimate from the best available meta-analysis. Not the transformative sleep fix the supplement industry sells. 17 minutes of faster sleep onset, in populations running below the RDA.

The CARDIA dataset (Zhang et al., Sleep 2022): 3,964 American adults tracked across 35 years. Highest quartile of magnesium intake: 36% lower odds of short sleep duration (OR=0.64). That's epidemiological association, not causal proof. But the signal across 35 years is meaningful.

The 2025 bisglycinate RCT (Schuster et al., Nature and Science of Sleep): 155 adults with poor sleep, 250 mg elemental magnesium as bisglycinate, 8 weeks. Effect size: Cohen's d = 0.2. Small. Statistically significant. The most rigorous glycinate-sleep trial to date.

The form table matters: magnesium oxide has roughly 4% bioavailability. It's the cheapest to manufacture and therefore the most common on pharmacy shelves. The most-cited sleep trial (Abbasi 2012) used oxide. Glycinate and citrate absorb via dedicated amino acid transporters — dramatically better absorption.

The threonate marketing oversells the data. The 2010 Slutsky paper that launched Magtein is in rats. The first human sleep RCT (Rountree 2024, n=80) produced positive results — and was funded by AIDP Inc., with Magtein supplied by the patent holder, Threotech. The 2025 independent Frontiers in Nutrition trial showed more mixed objective signals.

The PPI interaction that matters: omeprazole, pantoprazole, esomeprazole disrupt TRPM6/TRPM7 channels — the intestinal magnesium transporters. Long-term PPI users can develop hypomagnesemia even at adequate dietary intake. If you take a daily PPI, the form question is secondary to the drug interaction question.

Comparison to the gold standard: CBT-I produces Hedges' g = 0.87-1.1 on sleep onset latency. The best magnesium RCT produces Cohen's d = 0.2-0.47 on the same outcome. These are not comparable categories. Magnesium is not a substitute for sleep architecture intervention.
twitter.cta: 60+ magnesium-and-sleep studies, honest accounting: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/magnesium-for-sleep — Share if someone you know is taking oxide expecting glycinate results.

linkedin.hook:
The magnesium supplement most people buy is magnesium oxide — roughly 4% bioavailability. Most of it exits via the colon before reaching systemic circulation.

The trials showing sleep benefits used glycinate or citrate. The Magtein marketing is built on a 2010 rat study.

Here's what 60+ human studies actually show.
linkedin.body:
Magnesium is one of the more evidence-supported supplements in the sleep category — and one of the most frequently misdirected purchases in a pharmacy.

The honest baseline from the literature:

48% of American adults consume less magnesium than their Estimated Average Requirement. This is the reason the sleep signal exists: the effect concentrates in populations running below the RDA. If your dietary intake is already adequate, supplementation is unlikely to produce measurable sleep improvement.

The meta-analytic estimate: Mah & Pitre (BMC Complementary Medicine, 2021) — three RCTs, 151 older adults — found sleep onset latency fell by approximately 17 minutes versus placebo (p=0.0006). Evidence quality: low to very low. Their conclusion: the literature is too weak for clinical recommendations, but safety and low cost make supplementation worth considering in appropriate populations.

The 2025 RCT (Schuster et al., Nature and Science of Sleep): 155 adults with poor sleep, 250 mg elemental magnesium as bisglycinate, 8 weeks. Effect size: Cohen's d = 0.2 — the most rigorous bisglycinate-sleep trial to date, producing a small but statistically significant signal.

Form matters dramatically:
- Magnesium oxide: approximately 4% bioavailability in some estimates. The cheapest form and the most common on shelves. The most-cited sleep RCT (Abbasi 2012) used oxide.
- Magnesium glycinate/bisglycinate: absorbed via amino acid transporters, higher bioavailability, cleaner GI profile
- Magnesium citrate: consistently outperforms oxide in bioavailability in human crossover trials (Walker et al., 2003)
- Magnesium L-threonate: the 2010 rat paper (Slutsky, Neuron) is the foundation of the marketing. The first human sleep RCT was industry-funded. An independent 2025 Frontiers in Nutrition trial showed weaker objective signals.

The critical interaction: proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole, pantoprazole, esomeprazole) impair intestinal magnesium absorption via TRPM6/TRPM7 channel disruption. Long-term PPI users can develop hypomagnesemia regardless of dietary intake.

For context: CBT-I produces Hedges' g = 0.87-1.1 on sleep onset latency. The best magnesium RCT produces Cohen's d = 0.2-0.47. Different categories, different tools, different appropriate populations.

Full citation-backed breakdown: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/magnesium-for-sleep

slides.title: SLIDE 1 — HOOK
slides.body: The magnesium with the moon on the label is almost certainly oxide — 4% bioavailability. The sleep trials used glycinate. Threonate was tested in rats first. 60+ human studies tell a different story than the label.

slides.title: SLIDE 2 — STAT
slides.body: 48% of American adults consume less magnesium than their Estimated Average Requirement. That deficiency gap is why the sleep signal exists. If you're replete, supplementation probably doesn't move the needle.

slides.title: SLIDE 3 — REVEAL
slides.body: Mah & Pitre meta-analysis (BMC, 2021): sleep onset latency fell 17 minutes vs. placebo. Not the transformative fix the labels imply. 17 minutes, in deficient older adults, with low-to-very-low quality evidence.

slides.title: SLIDE 4 — DETAIL
slides.body: The 2025 bisglycinate RCT (Schuster et al., n=155): Cohen's d = 0.2. Small. Statistically significant. The most rigorous glycinate-sleep trial to date. Compare to CBT-I: Hedges' g = 0.87-1.1. Not the same league.

slides.title: SLIDE 5 — QUOTE
slides.body: "The effects of magnesium L-threonate (Magtein) on cognitive performance and sleep quality in adults" — The 2010 Slutsky paper cited in that marketing is a rat study. The human evidence is two trials, one industry-funded.

slides.title: SLIDE 6 — IMPLICATION
slides.body: Omeprazole, pantoprazole, esomeprazole disrupt the intestinal magnesium transporter. Long-term PPI users can develop deficiency even at adequate dietary intake. If you take a daily PPI, that interaction matters more than which form you pick.

slides.title: SLIDE 7 — SOURCES
slides.body: Mah & Pitre, BMC (2021) · Schuster et al., Nature and Science of Sleep (2025) · Abbasi et al., JRMS (2012) · Zhang et al., Sleep (2022) · Gommers et al., Acta Physiologica (2022)

reddit.title: What does the actual evidence say about magnesium for sleep — form, dose, who it works for, and why threonate marketing outpaces the data
reddit.body:
There's a lot of confused information about magnesium and sleep, so here's a breakdown of what the primary literature actually says.

WHO IT WORKS FOR:

The honest answer is: primarily people running below the magnesium RDA. 48% of U.S. adults consume less than their EAR. The Zhang 2022 CARDIA data (n=3,964, 35-year longitudinal): highest vs. lowest magnesium quartile showed 36% lower odds of short sleep duration. If you're already replete, supplementation is unlikely to move the needle.

HOW MUCH IT WORKS:

Mah & Pitre (BMC Complementary Medicine, 2021) meta-analysis — 3 RCTs, 151 older adults: sleep onset latency -17.36 min (p=0.0006). Evidence quality: low to very low. The 2025 Schuster bisglycinate RCT (n=155): Cohen's d = 0.2. Small, statistically significant. That's the honest effect estimate.

FOR COMPARISON: CBT-I (cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia) produces Hedges' g = 0.87-1.1 on sleep onset latency. Magnesium is not a substitute.

FORM MATTERS MORE THAN MOST THREADS ACKNOWLEDGE:

- Oxide: ~4% bioavailability. Most common pharmacy form because it's cheapest. The most-cited sleep study (Abbasi 2012) used oxide.
- Glycinate/bisglycinate: amino acid transport, higher bioavailability, least GI disturbance. The 2025 Schuster RCT used bisglycinate.
- Citrate: consistently outperforms oxide in bioavailability (Walker 2003 crossover trial)
- Threonate: the 2010 Slutsky paper is in rats. The first human sleep RCT (Rountree 2024, n=80) was industry-funded. An independent 2025 Frontiers in Nutrition trial showed weaker objective sleep signals.

PPI INTERACTION:

If you take omeprazole, pantoprazole, or esomeprazole daily, these disrupt TRPM6/TRPM7 magnesium transporters and can cause hypomagnesemia regardless of diet. Talk to a doctor before supplementing if you're a long-term PPI user.

PRACTICAL DOSE: 200-400mg elemental as glycinate or citrate, 45-60 min before bed. No head-to-head trial compares morning vs. evening timing.

Full breakdown: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/magnesium-for-sleep

reddit.title: Magnesium L-threonate for sleep: what the 2010 Slutsky paper actually is, and how the 2025 independent RCT changed the picture
reddit.body:
Magtein (magnesium L-threonate) has been a popular choice in nootropics circles, largely on the basis of the 2010 Slutsky et al. Neuron paper. I want to share a careful read of what the evidence chain actually looks like.

The Slutsky 2010 paper:
Rats. Oral MgT elevated brain magnesium concentrations and improved working memory, spatial memory, and long-term memory in both young and aged animals. The mechanism proposed: enhanced hippocampal synaptic density. Real finding in an animal model.

Slusky holds a patent on the compound. Magtein is manufactured by Threotech LLC.

Whether oral MgT meaningfully elevates brain magnesium in healthy humans at commercial doses: not established by independent peer-reviewed trials.

The first human sleep RCT (Rountree et al., Sleep Medicine: X, 2024):
n=80, 1g MgT daily (75mg elemental magnesium), 21 days. ISI scores dropped significantly vs. placebo. Deep sleep scores improved. Funded by AIDP Inc., authors affiliated with AIDP, Magtein supplied by Threotech.

The 2025 independent RCT (Arab et al., Frontiers in Nutrition):
Random assignment, double-blind, placebo-controlled. Results showed weaker objective sleep signals than the 2024 industry-funded trial. The gap between the two trials is the thing to watch as more independent work accumulates.

For comparison: the 2025 magnesium bisglycinate RCT (Schuster et al., Nature and Science of Sleep, n=155) — Cohen's d = 0.2. Independent. Not industry-funded.

Bottom line: threonate's sleep evidence consists of two human trials (one industry-funded, one showing weaker signals) and a 2010 rat study. Bisglycinate has one independent 2025 RCT with a small but real signal. Neither is impressive enough to justify a strong preference, but glycinate has the cleaner independence record.

Full context: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/magnesium-for-sleep

email.subject: The magnesium form that actually absorbs — and what 60 studies show
email.preheader: Oxide is 4% bioavailable. Threonate is a rat study. Glycinate has the cleanest human evidence. Here's the breakdown.
email.body:
The magnesium with the moon on the label at your pharmacy is almost certainly magnesium oxide — roughly 4% bioavailability. Most of it exits unabsorbed. The trials that showed sleep benefits used glycinate or citrate.

The honest meta-analytic estimate (Mah & Pitre, BMC 2021): sleep onset latency fell 17 minutes versus placebo in deficient older adults. Evidence quality: low to very low.

The 2025 bisglycinate RCT (Schuster et al., n=155): Cohen's d = 0.2. Small, statistically significant, the most rigorous glycinate-sleep trial to date.

The threonate marketing is built on a 2010 rat study. The first human sleep RCT was industry-funded. An independent 2025 Frontiers in Nutrition trial showed weaker objective signals.

48% of U.S. adults run below the magnesium EAR. If you're in that group and sleeping poorly, 200-400 mg elemental as glycinate or citrate before bed has a real but modest case behind it.

Full 60-study breakdown: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/magnesium-for-sleep

pullQuotes: Magnesium oxide: 4% bioavailability. The cheapest form is the most common on pharmacy shelves.
The honest meta-estimate: 17 minutes faster sleep onset. In deficient, older adults.
The Magtein marketing was built on a 2010 rat study. The human evidence is two trials.
48% of American adults fall below the magnesium EAR. That's why the signal exists.
CBT-I: g = 0.87. Magnesium: d = 0.2. These are not comparable interventions.
